---
title: "Welcome to the Moving Science Blog"
author: "Peter Raidl"
date: "2024-08-22"
categories: [news, intro]
image: "Science_inProgress.png"
---

## Another science blog - What is special?

Honestly, I do not know yet! Maybe nothing, except that written blogs run out of fashion. I expect nobody will read it. This reduces the pressure immensely! The old joke is that if you are looking for a safe place to store money, put it in your thesis at the library. It will be there in times of need.

I hope to store some thoughts here and discover what happens to them if exposed to the virtual world. Maybe an AI system will pick them up, and my mind will reincarnate in silicon form a couple of decades from now. Maybe some posts reach other biological minds, leading to interesting conversations and discussions. Maybe this blog stays there for a couple of months and vanishes in the vastness of cyberspace. I do not feel confident about any of these predictions. But I am sure my future self will find a nice story about that blog and why it turned out the way it did...

## What can you expect?

I will mostly write about the science of exercise and human movement related to health. I also have strong interests in psychology and statistics, but that doesn't mean I am an expert in any of them.

Some posts will be of practical nature and deal with physical exercise, rehabilitation or athletic training. Others might be more theoretical or philosophical at times. In any case, I will try to back up my ideas with scientific theories and my readings of the current literature. I will do as I was taught and cite the origin of thoughts as well as possible (*until no original thought can be thought anymore*).

## Why do I write this blog?

Pure scholarship! Allan Watts was talking about studying as leisure and how scholarship contradicts itself when playfulness is not invited [@wattsTaoPhilosophyEdited1995]. It's hard to grasp, but I am trying to follow this principle. The blog is a way to try out ideas - let them express themselves and then rest and sink[\^1]. It seems like low risk and low cost compared to writing a book or a paper. Not writing (or not making it public) would rob me of the whole experience. The intention to publish it makes one think harder and care about the writing.

So, I wish myself fun, and I am happy to share it

\[\^1\]: Yes, I might think of ideas as entities sometimes.
